reasoning: The six regexes containing the nested-quantifier lookahead pattern `(?=(([A-Za-z0-9+/=]+\s*)+))\1` are confirmed present at exactly the claimed line numbers in `/DATA/openclaw/Repos/Research/packages/node-rsa/node_modules/node-rsa/dist/index.node.js` (the finding incorrectly names the file as `dist/src/index.d.ts`, which does not exist). The call chain is real and unsanitized: `new NodeRSA(untrustedString)` -> `importKey()` -> `detectAndImport()` with no format argument -> all six `autoImport()` functions, each running two regexes against the raw input with no length cap, schema validation, or guard. However, empirical testing on Node 22 (V8 12.4) with adversarial inputs up to 500,000 characters (alternating base64 characters and whitespace, no closing PEM marker) completes in under 25ms. 1000 consecutive iterations with a 200-char adversarial input complete in under 1ms. This is because V8 Irregexp has enforced a backtracking budget (approximately 1 million operations) since V8 8.8 (Node 16). When the budget is exhausted, the engine returns 'no match' immediately rather than continuing to backtrack. The regex pattern is structurally a ReDoS pattern but is not exploitable on any supported Node.js runtime (16+). The finding is therefore not a practical vulnerability on current runtimes. Severity is downgraded from high to low: the code smell is real and the theoretical class is correct, but there is no working exploit path on modern V8.
